query: Senate committee cameras Supreme Court federal courtrooms bill status vote

mako.raw:
{"query":"Senate committee cameras Supreme Court federal courtrooms bill status vote","model":"mako","response_time_ms":1777,"verdict":null,"extra_info":null,"results":[{"url":"https://www.judiciary.senate.gov/press/rep/releases/judiciary-committee-advances-bipartisan-legislation-to-shine-light-on-federal-court-proceedings","title":"Judiciary Committee Advances Bipartisan ... | United States Senate Committee on the Judiciary","content":"WASHINGTON – The Senate Judiciary Committee today advanced two bipartisan bills by voice vote to allow television cameras in federal courtrooms.
The bills are led by Judiciary Committee Chairman Chuck Grassley (R-Iowa) and Ranking Member Dick Durbin (D-Ill.).
The Sunshine in the Courtroom Act, authored by Grassley and Sen.
Amy Klobuchar (D-Minn.), would grant all federal courts the discretion to allow television cameras in the courtroom, while protecting the identities of witnesses and jurors.
The Cameras in the Courtroom Act, led by Durbin and Grassley, would instruct the Supreme Court to permit television coverage of open sessions, unless a majority of the Justices decide that doing so would violate due process."},{"url":"https://fixthecourt.com/2026/06/fix-the-court-backed-cameras-bills-pass-senate-judiciary/","title":"Fix the Court-Backed Cameras Bills Pass Senate Judiciary | Fix the Court","content":"Two bipartisan court transparency bills that Fix the Court endorsed advanced out of the Senate Judiciary Committee today.
Finally and unsurprisingly, the Judicial Conference wrote a letter opposing the bill.
Durbin — a long-time champion of court transparency whose retirement is imminent — the Senate for the first time puts one or both of these bills on the floor for a vote."},{"url":"https://www.bestlawfirms.com/articles/cameras-supreme-court-congress-2026/8149","title":"Cameras at the Supreme Court: Congress's 2026 Push Explained","content":"8 days ago — Two Senate bills may open federal courts to cameras for the first time. History suggests the Supreme Court won't go quietly.Read more
Two bipartisan bills—the Cameras in the Courtroom Act and the Sunshine in the Courtroom Act—cleared the Senate Judiciary Committee in June, sponsored by Sens.
Legislation to televise Supreme Court oral arguments has cleared the Senate Judiciary Committee before, most recently in 2021.
Individual justices have argued that the public would struggle to understand complex arguments without context, expressed concern about losing personal privacy if recorded during public sessions, and worried that lawyers might play to the cameras while justices self-censor their questions.
It would require the Supreme Court to allow television coverage of its open sessions, with an exception if a majority of justices vote to suspend coverage to protect a party's due process rights in a specific case."},{"url":"https://www.law.com/nationallawjournal/2026/06/18/senate-panel-advances-bills-to-allow-cameras-in-us-supreme-court-lower-courts/","title":"Senate Panel Advances Bills to Allow Cameras in US Supreme Court, Lower Courts | Law.com","content":"Justice Amy Coney Barrett appeared alongside Justice Elena Kagan in dual hearings before House and Senate budget subcommittees Tuesday, the first time members of the Supreme Court have testified before the House since 2019 and the Senate since 2011."},{"url":"https://legis1.com/news/senate-court-transparency-ai-legislation","title":"Senate Committee Votes on Court Transparency & AI | Legis1","content":"The business meeting, scheduled for Thursday, June 11 puts the two court transparency bills and a landmark AI likeness bill either on a path toward the Senate floor, or stops them cold.
S.1133, the Sunshine in the Courtroom Act, and S.1146, the Cameras in the Courtroom Act, would open federal courtrooms, including the Supreme Court, to television cameras for the first time.
Meanwhile, S.4591, the NO FAKES Act of 2026, would create a new federal property right over every American's voice and visual likeness, with penalties reaching $750,000 per work for platforms that distribute unauthorized AI-generated replicas.
The committee will also vote on six judicial nominees and a nominee for Department of Justice Inspector General.
The June 11 business meeting is the first formal committee action either one has received in the 119th Congress.
Chuck Grassley chairs the Senate Judiciary Committee for the 119th Congress, with Dick Durbin as ranking member."}]}
